planting.note: Plant small bulbs (sets) in late summer to fall in well-drained, fertile soil; rakkyo grows through the cool season and is harvested in late spring. In mild climates (zone 8–11) it naturalizes as a perennial bulb and spreads freely. Space bulbs 3–4 inches apart to allow for multiplication.

directions: Plant small bulbs in fall in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; rakkyo (Allium chinense) is a small, perennial allium grown throughout Japan, China, and Southeast Asia specifically for pickling — one of Japan's most beloved preserved foods, pickled rakkyo (rakkyo no su-zuke) are the sweet-tart, crispy pickled bulbs served as a condiment alongside curry rice, bento boxes, and traditional Japanese meals. The small, elongated bulbs are harvested in early summer, cleaned, and pickled in vinegar with sugar and salt. They have a milder, more delicate onion flavor than regular onions, with a distinctive crispy texture retained through the pickling process. The plant itself is an attractive ornamental — the purple-pink flowers appear in fall on upright stems and are followed by the edible bulbs that multiply underground like shallots. In mild climates it naturalizes readily and provides bulbs year after year with minimal care.
sources: Asian Vegetable Research and Development Center

planting.note: Sow seeds in late summer or early fall for spring germination; Korean angelica requires cold stratification and germinates best with exposure to winter cold. Sow direct where plants will grow permanently, as the deep taproot makes transplanting difficult. Allow plants to self-sow to maintain a colony.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, fertile, humus-rich, partly shaded soil; Korean angelica is an exceptionally dramatic biennial or short-lived perennial from Korea and Japan that produces some of the most architecturally impressive flower heads in horticulture — enormous, domed umbels of deep burgundy-maroon flowers, 4–6 inches across, carried in large clusters at the top of stout, dramatically purple-flushed, hollow stems that can reach 6 feet. The entire plant is suffused with rich purple-maroon coloring — stems, leaf veins, flower stalks, and flower heads — making it unlike anything else in the garden. As a biennial, it germinates year one, flowers spectacularly in year two, sets abundant seed, and dies. In moist, partly shaded woodland garden settings it self-sows reliably to maintain a permanent colony. The flowers are magnets for butterflies, bumblebees, and other pollinators. Used medicinally in Korean traditional medicine.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in shade; Korean rock fern is compact enough for small gardens and containers. It tolerates dryness under trees better than many ferns once established and is semi-evergreen to evergreen in mild climates.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; Korean rock fern is one of the most compact, well-behaved, and versatile hardy ferns available, producing neat, glossy, dark-green, finely divided fronds that are more rigid and leathery than most ferns and remain semi-evergreen to evergreen in zones 7–9. The narrow, lance-shaped fronds have small, holly-like pinnae with spiny-toothed margins that give the frond a distinctive texture. It stays under 18 inches and is suitable for small gardens, containers, woodland borders, and shaded corners where larger ferns would overwhelm the space. Native to rocky woodland slopes of Japan, Korea, and China, it is more tolerant of dry shade than most ferns once established. Excellent for the front of a shaded border alongside hostas, hellebores, and woodland wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in shade to part shade; Wallich's wood fern is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled. The emerging croziers (fiddleheads) are strikingly covered with jet-black or dark-brown scales, creating a dramatic spring display before the fronds unfurl.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full to part shade; Wallich's wood fern is one of the most architecturally spectacular hardy ferns available, producing an upright, vase-shaped shuttlecock of long, elegant, yellow-green to deep-green fronds with prominent dark midribs and distinctive black to dark-brown scales on the rachis (midrib) that remain visible and ornamental throughout the growing season. The emerging croziers in spring are covered with striking jet-black scales, creating a dramatic architectural display weeks before the fronds unfurl. Each frond can reach 3–4 feet in length, forming bold clumps of impressive visual impact in the shade garden. Unlike many decorative ferns, it is also surprisingly tolerant of dry conditions once established, making it useful under trees where other ferns struggle. Native to mountain woodlands from the Himalayas to Japan and Africa.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring before new growth or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil; golden-scale male fern is more drought-tolerant than many ferns and succeeds in conditions ranging from deep, moist woodland soil to drier positions under tree canopy.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; golden-scale male fern is a robust, semi-evergreen European fern producing large, upright, medium-green fronds that remain attractive from spring through much of the winter in mild climates. The distinguishing feature is the prominent, golden-orange to rust-orange scales that densely cover the crowns, stipes, and midribs, creating a warm, glowing appearance particularly evident in early spring as new fronds emerge. The upright, shuttlecock growth habit is tidy and architectural. Distinguished cultivars include 'Cristata The King,' with elegant, crested frond tips, and 'Pinderi,' which forms a more refined, narrow frond. It is a reliable, long-lived garden fern that gradually forms impressive, multi-crowned specimens over many years. More drought-tolerant than most ferns once established, making it suitable for dry shade under large trees.
